data.title: Smith Micro outlines leadership transition and targets Q2 2026 non-GAAP profitability amid strong SafePath OS momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Smith Micro Software, Inc. (SMSI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • William Smith, Co-Founder, Chairman of the Board, President & CEO, emphasized, "Much of the work completed in 2025 has contributed to our progress. We have strengthened our product lineup with a strategic focus on phones in our SafePath OS solutions for kids and seniors. The senior-focused solution alone more than doubles our total addressable market." Smith highlighted that the company has rationalized its cost structure and is "building a culture of continuous improvement and operational efficiency." He stated, "We believe it will support an even further reduced loss in Q1 of 2026 and most importantly, non-GAAP profit in Q2 and beyond." Smith also announced an executive succession plan, transitioning to Executive Chairman after 44 years, with Timothy Huffmyer set to become President and CEO.

  • Timothy Huffmyer, VP, COO, CFO & Treasurer, stated, "We have recently completed several funding transactions. During the fourth quarter, the company received approximately $2.7 million of cash from a registered direct offering and private placement transaction." Huffmyer also announced, "Bethany Braund will serve as our new Chief Financial Officer" and described her qualifications and experience. He reiterated, "We are generally on track to achieve these savings in 2026. These efforts are part of our broader initiative to realign the company's cost structure with long-term business goals, strengthen the company's financial foundation and accelerate our path to profitability."

Outlook

  • Smith Micro projects consolidated revenues for Q1 2026 to be in the range of $4.2 million to $4.5 million. Gross margin for Q1 2026 is expected to be in the range of 76% to 78%, and once the full quarter of cost benefits is realized, the margin is expected to be between 78% to 80%. The company’s long-term gross margin target remains at 85%. Huffmyer stated, "We anticipate a further decline in non-GAAP operating expenses of 5% in the first quarter of 2026 as compared to the fourth quarter of 2025." Smith highlighted that the company plans to "bring two new carrier customers to the market by midyear 2026. Both customer wins are the result of our SafePath OS product offerings."

Financial Results

  • For Q4 2025, Smith Micro posted revenue of $4 million, compared to $5 million for Q4 2024 and $4.3 million for Q3 2025. Family Safety revenue for the quarter was $3.2 million, down from $3.8 million year-over-year and $3.6 million sequentially. CommSuite revenue was $800,000, compared to $1.1 million for Q4 2024 and flat sequentially. Gross profit for Q4 2025 was $3 million, down from $3.8 million in Q4 2024 and $3.2 million in Q3 2025. Gross margin for the quarter was 76.4%. GAAP operating expenses for Q4 2025 were $7.4 million, a decrease compared to prior periods. The GAAP net loss attributable to common stockholders was $4.7 million, or $0.20 loss per share. Non-GAAP net loss attributable to common stockholders was $2.1 million, or $0.09 loss per share. Cash and cash equivalents as of December 31, 2025, were $1.5 million.

Q&A

  • Matthew Harrigan, The Benchmark Company, LLC, Research Division: "Do you have any thoughts on what -- kind of the annual revenues, I mean you can kind of figure out where your margin is going to lay out, but the value of a normalized revenues with a major mobile carrier in the U.S., I mean, if you perform optimally... But what do you think the prospective revenue opportunity is, kind of brushing it with kind of a VC painting brush, if you will?" Timothy Huffmyer responded that investors should "think about the number of subs that are available or family subs that are available at the carrier... and then we've often guided on a fee or our revenue per unit as a couple of dollars per family unit... you could kind of run out that from a modeling standpoint."

  • Matthew Harrigan: "But you don't talk too much about competition... do you see things kind of added ad-hoc to other software solutions? Or what are people doing who aren't using you..." William Smith replied, "I think that's really the power of SafePath OS. And why we became so active around the phones... I think we have put ourselves in an excellent spot where we can really bring added value... I think that's why I think we're going to be very, very successful going forward. And I think that the phones are going to be the major differentiator."

  • Matthew Harrigan: "Are you seeing any better awareness of your product?" William Smith said, "We are looking forward to launching two new carrier customers midyear. Both are being driven by SafePath OS... Europe is more of a greenfield opportunity for us... this makes the decision process by those carriers that much easier."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for quantitative sizing of the opportunity and competitive differentiation, but maintained a constructive tone, expressing interest in the company’s market positioning and product traction.

  • Management’s tone in the prepared remarks and Q&A was confident and optimistic, with repeated references to being "bullish" on the future and a "strong growing pipeline." Smith stated, "I am more than excited and extremely confident as I look ahead to 2026 and beyond." Huffmyer also described the team as "amazing and extremely dedicated." In Q&A, management continued to emphasize leadership and differentiation, particularly around SafePath OS for phones.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a positive to slightly more optimistic tone, bolstered by the succession plan, new funding, and a clearer path to profitability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured significant management changes, including the announced CEO transition and appointment of a new CFO, compared to no such changes in Q3 2025.

  • Product strategy remains focused on SafePath OS for both kids and seniors, with a new emphasis on phones as a differentiator and expansion of the addressable market. The company now plans to bring two new carrier customers to market by mid-2026, an explicit target not detailed last quarter.

  • Revenue declined compared to the previous quarter, attributed to a delayed feature launch and a one-time event with a customer deployment. However, the company continued to reduce operating expenses and reinforced guidance for achieving non-GAAP profitability by Q2 2026.

  • The analyst focus shifted from cost reductions and pipeline growth to quantifying market opportunity and competitive positioning, reflecting increasing interest in the company’s market expansion.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a one-time revenue event with an existing deployment, which temporarily impacted Q4 2025 revenue but stated that "All revenue associated with this event has resumed to normal levels during the first quarter of 2026."

  • There is ongoing pressure to achieve further cost reductions and maintain operational efficiency while investing in innovation.

  • Analysts raised concerns about the competitive landscape and the risk of carriers developing in-house solutions, which management addressed by emphasizing SafePath OS’s differentiation, especially with device integration.

Final Takeaway

Smith Micro Software’s Q4 2025 call highlighted a pivotal management transition, a sharpened focus on SafePath OS for both kids and seniors, and a cost structure realignment supporting the company's goal for non-GAAP profitability by Q2 2026. With two new carrier launches targeted for midyear and an expanded addressable market, management expressed strong confidence in the business’s direction and the potential for renewed growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sight Sciences outlines $82M–$88M 2026 revenue target while expanding interventional dry eye strategy
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sight Sciences (SGHT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul Badawi stated that the company “ended 2025 with solid execution across our business, highlighted by fourth quarter revenue growth in both segments, strong gross margins and continued operating expense discipline and cash management.” He emphasized a “clear strategy to return to double-digit growth while maintaining our operational rigor and financial discipline” in 2026, spotlighting proprietary technologies OMNI and TearCare for glaucoma and dry eye disease, respectively.
  • Badawi highlighted a key strategic update: “What we previously referred to as surgical glaucoma and dry eye, we now call Interventional Glaucoma and Interventional Dry Eye, reflecting our focus on elevating the standards of care with earlier procedure-based interventions.”
  • The CEO noted a “very important reimbursement milestone” for TearCare, as Novitas and First Coast Service Options established pricing for CPT code 0563T, calling it “a turning point for our TearCare business model.” He cited Interventional Dry Eye Q4 revenue of $0.7 million, driven by sales to approximately 80 accounts, with about 30 being new engagements.
  • On Interventional Glaucoma, Badawi said, “Revenue was $19.7 million, up 5% year-over-year and flat sequentially… ordering accounts increased 2% compared to the prior year.” He pointed to targeted investments in stand-alone market education and commercialization as growth drivers for 2026.
  • CFO Jim Rodberg stated, “In the fourth quarter, total revenue was $20.4 million, a 7% increase... Gross margin was 87%, consistent with the prior year.” He also noted a decrease in total operating expenses to $21.5 million and reported a net loss of $4.2 million or $0.08 per share. Rodberg emphasized, “With the operating discipline and cost structure we need to support growth, and over time, we believe this positions us to achieve cash flow breakeven without the need to raise additional equity capital.”

Outlook

  • The company initiated 2026 revenue guidance of $82 million to $88 million, reflecting 6% to 14% growth over 2025. Interventional Glaucoma segment guidance is $77 million to $81 million, representing 2% to 7% growth, and Interventional Dry Eye is guided for $5 million to $7 million compared to $1.6 million in 2025.
  • CFO Rodberg provided: “We are also initiating our guidance expectations for full year 2026, adjusted operating expenses of $93 million to $96 million, representing an increase of 6% to 9% compared to 2025.”
  • Management described 2026 guidance as “prudent targets” and expects Interventional Glaucoma Q1 revenue to grow low single digits year-over-year, with first quarter being the lowest of 2026 for that segment.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 total revenue was $20.4 million. Interventional Glaucoma contributed $19.7 million and Interventional Dry Eye $0.7 million. Gross margin was 87% overall; Interventional Glaucoma gross margin was 88% and Interventional Dry Eye gross margin improved to 68%.
  • Total operating expenses declined to $21.5 million from $28.5 million, reflecting a cost structure adjustment after the August 2025 reduction in force. Adjusted operating expenses were $18.9 million.
  • The company ended Q4 2025 with $92 million in cash and cash equivalents and $40 million in debt. Cash usage was $0.4 million in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street Capital Markets: Asked for detail on low-end vs. high-end guidance assumptions for both segments. Badawi responded that Interventional Glaucoma operates in a “much more stable market and reimbursement environment,” and on Interventional Dry Eye, “we haven't assumed additional market access wins within our guidance, but the team is certainly heavily focused on market access initiatives.”
  • Danielle Antalffy, UBS: Inquired about the ramp and obstacles of the stand-alone glaucoma market. Badawi explained, “We're excited to be making some targeted investments in activating the stand-alone market... we've made some dedicated pseudophakic market development commercial investments, about half a dozen market development focused professionals at Sight Sciences right now.”
  • Steven Lichtman, William Blair: Queried about balancing spend and growth for dry eye. Rodberg stated, “The bulk of [investments] are on commercial infrastructure... on the market access side and driving market access progress and then also on the commercial infrastructure side.” Bauerlein added, “We see the Interventional Dry Eye opportunity as such a compelling large market opportunity and the early traction that we're seeing with accounts has validated that with us.”
  • Thomas Stephan, Stifel: Asked about TearCare’s peak sales potential. Bauerlein said, “We aren't going to quantify that today. But you can very quickly do some math that shows this is an incredible opportunity for us from a revenue perspective.”
  • Adam Maeder, Piper Sandler: Sought clarity on payer conversations for dry eye and reimbursement on glaucoma. Bauerlein reported progress: “We do expect to have more MACs join and have more MACs established fee seals, and we would expect that to happen this year.” Badawi, on goniotomy code changes, indicated that “if there's pressure on goniotomy alone as a procedure... that could actually be a tailwind at that time.”
  • David Saxon, Needham: Asked about differences in selling approaches to ophthalmologists vs. optometrists for TearCare. Bauerlein said, “We're still very much in early days with this... the accounts that we're engaging with now truly are the early visionaries.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with questions focused on growth assumptions, reimbursement expansion, and commercial strategy. Tone was constructive but probing, especially regarding guidance conservatism and growth barriers.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing “strong foundation,” “well positioned,” and “prudent targets.” In Q&A, management maintained positivity but occasionally deferred on specifics, such as not quantifying TAM for TearCare or exact timing of payer wins.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management tone remained confident and focused on execution. Analysts’ tone in both quarters was curious and cautiously optimistic, with some increased focus on the pace of dry eye adoption and commercial scaling in Q4.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter’s guidance for 2026 at $82 million to $88 million marks an explicit return to growth targets, following the previous quarter’s raised 2025 guidance to $76 million to $78 million.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from foundational reimbursement wins to scaling commercialization and growing market access, particularly for Interventional Dry Eye.
  • Management continues to stress operating discipline, but now with a more proactive stance on investing for growth in both segments.
  • Analysts’ focus has moved from congratulating initial reimbursement milestones to scrutinizing the scalability and execution of growth strategies.
  • Previously discussed management changes have transitioned to implementation, with new roles now established and discussed in the context of operational execution.

Risks and Concerns

  • Challenges cited include the early stage of Interventional Dry Eye market adoption and the need to expand reimbursement beyond the two initial MACs. Management noted, “we haven't assumed additional market access wins within our guidance.”
  • Potential future fee reductions for goniotomy procedures could impact the glaucoma segment, but management believes OMNI’s broader procedural capability may mitigate this.
  • Cash discipline and the ability to scale commercial infrastructure in line with market access expansion were highlighted as ongoing challenges, with CFO Rodberg noting the company’s “strong balance sheet to go fuel that growth.”
  • Analyst concerns centered on scalability, reimbursement timing, and the sustainability of average selling prices in Dry Eye.

Final Takeaway

Sight Sciences management describes 2025 as a year of transition toward scalable growth, marked by operating discipline, a clear strategy to pursue double-digit revenue increases, and a sharpened focus on commercial execution in both Interventional Glaucoma and Interventional Dry Eye. With major reimbursement milestones achieved for TearCare and a strong cash position, the company is initiating 2026 guidance with a significant growth target and investing in commercial infrastructure to capitalize on early market traction. The leadership team remains confident in their ability to expand market access, grow share, and drive scale while maintaining a disciplined approach to costs and operational rigor.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Baytex Energy President/COO Lundberg to succeed Greager as CEO
data.description:
Baytex Energy (BTE) said Wednesday it appointed current President and COO Chad Lundberg as its new CEO, succeeding Eric Greager, following its annual general meeting on May 7.

Lundberg became the company's President and COO in 2025 after serving as COO since July 2021; he joined Baytex (BTE) in 2018 as VP of Light Oil after holding executive positions at Raging River Exploration and Crescent Point Energy.

Greager joined Baytex (BTE) as CEO in 2022 after serving as President and CEO of Civitas Resources during 2018-22; he has 30 years of energy operations and management experience, including time at Encana, Dominion Resources, and Helmerich & Payne.

Baytex (BTE) -8.3% post-market Wednesday after reporting a Q4 loss of $1.12/share on a 14% Y/Y decline in revenues to $331M.

data.title: Baytex Energy GAAP EPS of -C$1.12, revenue of C$759.82M
data.description:
• Baytex Energy press release (BTE): Q4 GAAP EPS of -C$1.12.
  • Revenue of C$759.82M (-25.5% Y/Y).

  • Our 2026 budget, released in December 2025, targets annual production of 67,000 to 69,000 boe/d, representing 3% to 5% organic growth year-over-year, with E&D expenditures of $550 to $625 million. This plan is designed to deliver disciplined growth while investing in the long-term infrastructure and exploration to support future value creation. We have significant inventory depth and optionality across our portfolio to support our current plan and potentially accelerate growth beyond these levels.

  • We are efficiently executing our first quarter capital program with seven rigs currently active across our portfolio. Production in Q1/2026 is forecast to average 68,000 to 69,000 boe/d, with production increasing to approximately 70,000 boe/d as we exit 2026.

data.title: Verastem outlines 2026 milestones with CO-PACK franchise set to be self-sustaining in H2
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Verastem, Inc. (VSTM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Daniel Paterson, President and CEO, stated that 2025 was a “truly transformative year for Verastem as we transition to a commercial stage company following our FDA approval of the first treatment specifically for KRAS-mutated recurrent low-grade serous ovarian cancer, nearly two months ahead of our PDUFA date.” He reported $30.9 million in net product revenue for the launch period of May through December 2025 and $17.5 million for the fourth quarter. Paterson addressed the updated NCCN guidelines, noting they did not expand the recommendation to KRAS wild-type patients, but emphasized, “This does not change our launch trajectory.” He confirmed ongoing commitment to advancing clinical evidence and highlighted the early commercial success and market adoption of AVMAPKI FAKZYNJA CO-PACK. Paterson also announced progress in the RAMP 205 trial in pancreatic cancer and the advancement of the VS-7375 pipeline, stating, "Given the early results, we believe VS-7375 has the strong potential to be the preferred agent in treating KRAS G12D driven cancers."
  • Chief Commercial Officer Michael Crowther discussed launch performance, sharing that nearly 300 prescribers have adopted CO-PACK by February, more than half of prescriptions are from academic settings, and about 75% of target institutions have adopted the therapy. He said, “The team is executing well against all 3 key strategic launch imperatives, effectively reaching health care providers, ensuring seamless access to coverage and engaging and supporting patients throughout the journey.”
  • President of Development Dr. Michael Kauffman reported completion of enrollment in RAMP 301 and RAMP 205 ahead of schedule and highlighted the 57% overall response rate in KRAS mutant and 22% in KRAS wild-type from the Japan-specific RAMP 201J study. He noted the expansion of the VS-7375 program, with dose escalation ongoing and a focus on disease-specific Phase II trials following FDA guidance.
  • CFO Daniel Calkins stated, “We reported $17.5 million in net product revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 and $30.9 million for the full year… Cost of sales were $2.6 million for the fourth quarter,” and reaffirmed the cash position of $205 million at year-end 2025, with a pro forma cash balance of $234.4 million including warrant proceeds. He added, “We believe the LGSOC franchise will be self-sustaining in the second half of this year with CO-PACK revenues funding both the commercial operations and any ongoing clinical trials.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated a focus on maximizing CO-PACK adoption, generating registration-directed data for VS-7375 in major KRAS G12D solid tumors, executing the RAMP 301 confirmatory trial, and maintaining disciplined capital management. Paterson stated, “We want to maximize adoption of the CO-PACK to ensure every appropriate patient benefits from this novel treatment at their first reoccurrence.” The company expects to share updates on the RAMP 205 expansion cohort in Q2 2026 and preliminary data for VS-7375 in the first half of 2026, with more comprehensive data on VS-7375 anticipated in the second half of the year.

Financial Results

  • Verastem reported $17.5 million in net product revenue for Q4 2025 and $30.9 million for May–December 2025. Cost of sales for Q4 were $2.6 million, with R&D expenses at $31.7 million and SG&A at $24.4 million. Non-GAAP adjusted net loss was $39.8 million (or $0.48 per share diluted) for Q4. Year-end cash, cash equivalents and investments were $205 million, with a pro forma cash balance of $234.4 million including warrant proceeds. The company expects the LGSOC franchise to be self-sustaining in H2 2026.

Q&A

  • Eric Schmidt, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about NCCN guideline decisions and reimbursement for wild-type patients. Daniel Paterson replied, “We don't see any difference to how we've been running our business and to the trajectory… our team has been doing a wonderful job securing reimbursement regardless of KRAS mutation status.”
  • Michael Schmidt, Guggenheim: Inquired about VS-7375 safety and dose selection strategies. Dr. Kauffman responded, “The number of dose modifications is low and slower than typically that I expect in various trials… we're really pleased with what we're seeing.”
  • Yuxi Dong, Jefferies: Sought details on plans to reengage NCCN and FDA feedback on trial design. Paterson confirmed ongoing engagement and Dr. Kauffman explained that FDA requested separate protocols for registration-directed trials.
  • Graig Suvannavejh, Mizuho: Asked about prescribing dynamics and future financing. Paterson stated, “We believe that launch for the [ A+F ] franchise will be self-sustaining by the second half of the year and won't require additional fundraising.”
  • Leonid Timashev, RBC: Focused on 7375 safety and formulation improvements. Dr. Kauffman addressed side effect management and ongoing formulation evaluations.
  • Yuan Zhi, B. Riley: Inquired about protocol updates and dose titration. Dr. Kauffman clarified changes were administrative with no significant timeline impact and dose titration may not be required based on current data.
  • Matthew Venezia, Alliance Global: Asked about next steps in Japan for A+D combo and payer coverage for wild-type patients. Paterson discussed efforts for conditional approval in Japan and noted strong success in securing payer coverage for wild-type cases.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, seeking clarifications on trial progress, safety, reimbursement, and market adoption, with no overt skepticism or criticism.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident, emphasizing commercial and clinical progress. In Q&A, management remained measured and constructive, responding thoroughly to concerns, and at times expressing disappointment over guideline decisions but reiterating confidence in strategy.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management continued a steady, constructive dialogue, with a slight increase in confidence from management regarding financial sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw explicit confirmation of the CO-PACK franchise’s path to self-sustainability in H2 2026 and an extension of the cash runway into H1 2027, compared to the prior quarter’s projection of runway into the second half of 2026.
  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize growing commercial self-sufficiency and advancing the VS-7375 pipeline with clear FDA guidance for Phase II trials.
  • Analysts’ focus moved from early launch dynamics and initial safety signals to more detailed questions on protocol design, payer coverage for wild-type patients, and next steps in international markets.
  • Management’s confidence in the commercial and clinical outlook increased as commercial adoption broadened and clinical milestones were achieved ahead of schedule.

Risks and Concerns

  • The updated NCCN guidelines did not expand recommendations to KRAS wild-type patients, which management acknowledged but stated does not change the commercial trajectory.
  • Management highlighted the need for continued reimbursement efforts for wild-type patients and administrative changes to clinical protocols in response to FDA feedback.
  • Analysts raised questions about long-term safety, formulation improvements, and payer coverage for off-label or wild-type indications.
  • Management noted ongoing resource prioritization and a focus on non-dilutive financing options as key risk mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Verastem management highlighted 2025 as a transformative year with robust commercial launch execution, accelerated clinical progress in both LGSOC and KRAS G12D-driven cancers, and a strengthened cash position. The company is positioned to achieve self-sustaining operations for the CO-PACK franchise by the second half of 2026 and has outlined clear upcoming milestones in both commercial and R&D activities, reinforcing confidence in their strategic direction for 2026 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Pulmonx outlines 2026 revenue target of $90M-$92M with cost realignment and renewed U.S. sales focus
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Pulmonx Corporation (LUNG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Glendon French noted, “During the past few months, Derrick and I have taken a deliberate bottom-up approach to assess the business, building on what's working, addressing what isn't and better aligning our spending with our strategic goals.” French highlighted that Pulmonx has realigned its cost structure, prioritized high-return programs, and emphasized a renewed focus on U.S. sales growth, expansion of clinical initiatives, and maintaining financial leverage to move toward profitability.
  • French detailed operational missteps in 2025, including “the U.S. sales organization was stretched across too many competing initiatives,” disruptive changes to Territory Manager roles, and suboptimal sales incentives, leading to significant turnover.
  • He announced changes including direct oversight of U.S. sales by the CEO, new leadership in clinical affairs to accelerate AeriSeal’s CONVERT II trial, and a refined commercial strategy centered on key treating physicians and operational excellence in established centers.
  • French stated, “We have much greater visibility into what went wrong last year, and we have already begun taking decisive action to fix it.”
  • CFO Derrick Sung reported, “We recently executed a cost restructuring initiative that reduced our ongoing operating expenses by over 10%.” Sung also referenced a $60 million credit facility with a 5-year interest-only structure, which “meaningfully strengthens our balance sheet by extending the maturity of our existing debt out to 2031 and by providing us with access to additional undrawn capital.”

Outlook

  • Pulmonx expects full year 2026 revenue in the range of $90 million to $92 million, anticipating a return to year-over-year growth in both U.S. and international businesses in the back half of the year.
  • The company expects gross margin for 2026 to be approximately 75%, with operating expenses forecasted between $113 million and $115 million, inclusive of about $21 million in noncash stock-based compensation.
  • Management guidance incorporates minimal China sales in the first half of 2026, with shipments expected to resume in the back half following certificate renewal.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the company now provides more specific revenue and operating expense targets and greater detail on operational timing and market recovery.

Financial Results

  • Total worldwide revenue for Q4 2025 was $22.6 million, a 5% decrease from the same period last year.
  • Full year 2025 worldwide revenue reached $90.5 million, an 8% increase over the prior year.
  • U.S. revenue in Q4 2025 was $14.1 million, while international revenue was $8.5 million.
  • Gross margin for Q4 2025 was 77.6%. Operating expenses for the quarter were $27.4 million, down 11% year-over-year.
  • Net loss for Q4 2025 was ($10.4 million), or ($0.25) per share. Adjusted EBITDA loss was $5.5 million.
  • Cash, cash equivalents and marketable securities stood at $69.8 million at year-end 2025.

Q&A

  • John Young, Canaccord: Asked about turnover in the sales force and timing of new hires. CEO French responded that turnover “was really across the entire year, so it wasn't in the fourth quarter. The magnitude was directionally on the order of half of the sales organization across the year.”
  • Young: Inquired about changes to sales incentives. French explained, “We have been very careful, both on the design of the sales incentive plan and with the allocation of those quotas... Last year was the anomaly in terms of the construct, and frankly, we paid a price for that.”
  • Jason Bednar, Piper Sandler: Questioned why U.S. growth wouldn’t recover sooner. French replied, “We're springing off of somewhat spongy ground to begin with... we want to be careful given sort of the soft ground that we're springing off of here coming off of the fourth quarter and the average tenure of the sales force being quite a bit different at this point this year as it was last year.”
  • Bednar: Requested detail on cost reductions. Sung noted, “The majority of the costs that we took out... were from G&A and marketing. The sales force was not directly impacted by the cost restructuring.”
  • Annie (Stifel): Asked about balancing AeriSeal clinical trial investments with U.S. sales. French said, “We're trying to get things optimized and aligned to deliver on... the rate of clinical enrollment. I also don't think that, that's going to have a meaningful impact on the overall spend of the company.”
  • Annie (Stifel): Sought longer-term growth perspective. French stated, “I think without a question, our intention is to take our growth profile to a much better place exactly where that is. I think we'll learn a lot about that as we go through the year.” Sung added that guidance implies a return to double-digit growth by Q4 2026.
  • Nelson (Lake Street): Asked about market fundamentals. French described the pulmonary market as “increasingly active,” with macro elements being “a net positive over time.”
  • Simran (Wells Fargo): Asked about U.S. sales rep ramp and OUS contributions. Sung responded, “We normally look at kind of 6 to 9 months for a new rep or a new territory to get up the productivity curve.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were mostly neutral, probing for specifics on sales force alignment, productivity ramp, cost control, and international contributions, while expressing some skepticism about the pace of U.S. recovery and sustainability of cost reductions.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, with French stating, “We have much greater visibility into what went wrong last year, and we have already begun taking decisive action to fix it.” Sung conveyed confidence in achieving operating leverage and managing cash burn.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from tentative and early-stage assessment to assertive, with more detailed action plans and revised guidance. Analysts remained cautious but acknowledged improvements in communication and operational transparency.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter provided concrete guidance for 2026, while the previous quarter focused on assessment and planning.
  • Strategic focus has shifted from broad evaluation to targeted execution: direct CEO oversight of U.S. sales, a streamlined incentive plan, and clear priorities in commercial strategy.
  • Management’s confidence increased, moving from uncertainty to actionable plans and measurable goals.
  • Key metric changes include a decrease in Q4 revenue, but full year revenue rose, and cost controls have reduced operating expenses and cash burn.
  • Analysts’ tone remains measured, though they noted progress in management’s operational clarity and willingness to address internal challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted internal executional missteps as the primary cause of 2025 underperformance, including sales force turnover and misaligned incentives.
  • Risks related to China include delayed certificate renewal and minimal first-half sales, addressed by guidance timing.
  • Enrollment pace in the AeriSeal CONVERT II trial and ramp-up of new sales hires represent ongoing operational risks.
  • Management’s mitigation strategies include realigned cost structure, direct operational oversight, and a refined focus on high-impact sales initiatives.

Final Takeaway

Pulmonx’s leadership outlined a focused plan for 2026, emphasizing U.S. sales force stabilization, cost discipline, and clinical trial acceleration, with clear financial goals and operational benchmarks. Management communicated stronger confidence in execution, highlighted by direct CEO involvement in sales, a $60 million credit facility, and targeted investments in growth initiatives, while acknowledging that meaningful U.S. revenue growth is expected in the latter half of the year as new hires ramp up. The company projects year-end momentum, aiming for double-digit global growth by Q4 2026, supported by streamlined operations and an expanded addressable market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Morgan Stanley to cut about 3% of workforce across key divisions
data.description:
Morgan Stanley (MS) is eliminating roughly 3% of its global workforce, or about 2,500 jobs, The Wall Street Journal reported Wednesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

Shares of Morgan Stanley (MS) have risen about 38% in the past 12 months.

The layoffs are affecting employees across the bank’s three main business units: investment banking and trading, wealth management and investment management. The reductions reflect a combination of evolving business priorities, geographic adjustments and individual performance considerations. The cuts are taking place in both the U.S. and international offices.

Many of the job reductions occurred Wednesday, though the process began last week, the Journal reported. Morgan Stanley has conducted several rounds of staff reductions in recent years.

Within the wealth-management unit, the layoffs include private bankers and certain operational roles. Some affected employees were involved in providing mortgages and related lending services to wealthy clients.

The staffing changes come after a strong financial year for the firm. Morgan Stanley (MS), which employs roughly 83,000 people, reported record annual revenue in 2025 from its investment banking and trading business as well as from its wealth-management division.

Industrywide, large financial institutions experienced one of their strongest years in 2025, fueled by a rebound in corporate dealmaking, active trading during volatile markets and continued spending by wealthy clients.

Morgan Stanley’s (MS) wealth-management unit, which typically generates close to half of the firm’s overall revenue, reported a 13% increase in revenue in the fourth quarter.

The layoffs also come amid a broader wave of white-collar job cuts across corporate America. Many companies have pointed to productivity gains from artificial intelligence as a factor behind workforce reductions.

Last month, Jack Dorsey said Block (XYZ) would cut about 40% of its workforce, more than 4,000 employees, arguing that rapidly improving AI systems are enabling the company to operate with fewer staff. Some analysts have questioned that explanation, suggesting the move may primarily reflect an effort to reduce costs at an organization seen as overstaffed.

Other technology companies have also cited AI as part of restructuring efforts. Salesforce (CRM) eliminated roughly 4,000 customer-support roles last year, while Pinterest (PINS) has said it plans to cut nearly 15% of its workforce as it reallocates resources toward AI-related positions.

data.title: Baker Hughes aims to sell $10B in cross-border bond sale - Bloomberg
data.description:
Baker Hughes (BKR) is planning to raise ~$10B from a cross-border bond sale as soon as this week to help fund its acquisition of Chart Industries, Bloomberg reported Wednesday.

The oilfield services company has mandated banks led by Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley to arrange calls with investors, and an offering of euro- and dollar- denominated bonds may follow, according to the report.

Baker Hughes agreed to buy industrial equipment maker Chart in a $9.6B deal announced in July, expanding its reach into liquefied natural gas, data centers, and other technologies.

Proceeds from the bond sale reportedly would replace a 364-day loan facility of as much as $14.9B signed last year to help finance the acquisition.

data.title: Exchange Income prices inaugural $600M investment-grade senior unsecured notes
data.description:
• Exchange Income (EIF:CA) on Wednesday priced a $600M offering of 4.324% senior unsecured notes due March 13, 2031.
  • The net proceeds from the offering are expected to be used to repay existing indebtedness under the corporation’s credit facilities and for general corporate purposes.
  • The offering is being led by RBC Capital Markets, CIBC Capital Markets, and National Bank Capital Markets as joint lead agents and active bookrunners.
  • The offering is expected to close on or about March 13, 2026.
  • Press release.
